prompts: A 2-week sprint is mid-flight when a phase gate arrives in a hybrid stage-gate + agile model. Which ceremony must NOT be skipped — the one that produces the reviewed increment the gate's go/no-go decision depends on?

correct.why: The sprint review demonstrates the working increment to stakeholders, and that reviewed increment is exactly what the gate's go/no-go decision needs.

distractors.trap: Routine coordination for the team — it does not produce the reviewed increment a gate needs.
Improves how the team works, but it is internal and not what the gate decision depends on.
A casual chat is no substitute for a formal, demonstrable increment review.

explanation: A phase gate's go/no-go needs a reviewed, demonstrable increment. That is what the sprint review produces, so it is the ceremony you protect when a gate lands mid-sprint; standups, retros and informal chats do not feed the gate.

prompts: Cost performance has been stable (CPI steady) and the overruns are expected to continue at the current rate. Which EAC formula should the PM use to forecast?

correct.why: When current cost efficiency is typical and will continue, dividing the budget by CPI extends that trend to completion.

distractors.trap: Assumes the overrun was a one-off and the rest runs to plan — not the case when the trend continues.
Used when BOTH cost and schedule variances persist; here only cost efficiency is in play.
Not a standard EAC forecast — it just offsets the budget by the current cost variance.

explanation: EAC = BAC/CPI is the forecast when the current cost performance is representative and expected to continue — it projects the observed efficiency across the remaining work.

prompts: A one-off event caused the cost overrun, and the remaining work is expected to run at the originally planned rate. Which EAC formula fits?

correct.why: When the variance was atypical, add the remaining budgeted work (BAC − EV) to actual cost at face value — no efficiency factor applied.

distractors.trap: Assumes the overrun continues at the current rate — wrong when it was a one-off.
Deflates remaining work by both indices; only used when cost and schedule pressure persist.
Not a valid EAC formula — EAC combines actual cost with an estimate to complete, not a product like this.

explanation: When the overrun is a one-off and future work runs to plan, EAC = AC + (BAC − EV): actual cost so far plus the remaining budgeted work at its planned value.

prompts: Both cost and schedule are under pressure and both variances are expected to persist through the rest of the project. Which EAC formula best reflects this?

correct.why: When cost AND schedule performance will both continue to drag, the remaining work is deflated by both indices together.

distractors.trap: Accounts for cost performance only — ignores the persisting schedule pressure.
Assumes the rest runs to plan — wrong when both variances continue.
Not a standard EAC forecast and uses only the schedule index.

explanation: When both cost and schedule variances will persist, EAC = AC + (BAC − EV)/(CPI × SPI) applies both performance indices to the remaining work — the most pessimistic of the standard forecasts.
